title: Chapter 14: Coffee Shop Closure
content: The coffee shop was different, and it took her a moment to understand why.

Same wooden counter. Same chalk menu board with the smudged specials. Same mismatched furniture that looked like it had been salvaged from three different estate sales. But the person behind the counter was new — a young man with short hair and a tattoo of a crescent moon on his wrist, moving between the espresso machine and the milk frother with the easy competence of someone who'd been doing this a while.
Not Eli.
Wren stood in the doorway. The bell above the door didn't ring when she entered. She wasn't sure if it was broken or if she was.
She approached the counter. The new barista glanced up, pleasant and blank.
"What can I get you?"
"The usual," Wren said.
His expression didn't flicker. No spark of recognition, no polite confusion, just the waiting face of someone serving a stranger.
"I'm sorry — what's the usual?"

The question wasn't accusatory. It wasn't suspicious. It was genuinely blank. He had never seen her before in his life. There was no slot in his memory labeled *customer who comes in every Tuesday and Thursday, sits in the corner, orders Americano with an extra shot, leaves sketches on napkins*.
That slot had never existed.
"Eli," she said. "Where's Eli?"
"Who?"
"The other barista. Worked here before you."
He wiped the counter with a cloth, thinking. "I've been here six weeks. It was just me and Dara when I started. There's no Eli on the schedule."

Six weeks. Wren had been coming here for over a year. Eli had known her name. Eli had once given her a cookie on the house because she looked tired. Eli had watched her draw on napkins and said, "You should do something with that," and she'd laughed and said, "Like what?" and he'd said, "Like anything. You're good."
That conversation had happened. She remembered it. The warmth of the cookie. The scratch of the napkin under her pen. The fluorescent light catching the steam off her coffee.
But Eli was gone, and this new barista had never heard of him, and the world was editing itself around her like a sculptor chiseling away everything that proved she'd been here.
"Americano," she said quietly. "Extra shot."

He made it without comment. She paid — cash, because the card was useless. The register accepted the bills without trouble. At least cash still worked. At least paper money didn't care if you existed.

Her corner was still there. The small table by the window with the wobbly leg and the view of the crosswalk. She sat down and wrapped her hands around the cup. The heat seeped into her fingers — the first warmth she'd felt all day.

The shop was quiet for a mid-afternoon. Two women at a table near the door, heads bent together over a laptop. A man in paint-splattered work pants reading a paperback. The barista wiping down the espresso machine, humming something tuneless.
Normal. Aggressively normal.

Wren sipped the Americano and tasted nothing. The bitterness was there — she could identify it, the way you can identify a word in a language you no longer speak — but it didn't register as taste. More like data. *Bitter. Hot. Coffee.*
She set the cup down and pulled a napkin from the dispenser.

The pen was in her pocket — the same one she'd been carrying for weeks, capped and uncapped so many times that the plastic was wearing smooth. She uncapped it now and held it over the napkin without drawing.

The corner table. The window. The crosswalk outside with its reliable rhythm of walk signals and brake lights. She'd drawn this a hundred times. Quick strokes, the architecture of the street, the shapes of people in motion. Her hand knew the angles by heart.
She drew the coffee shop instead. The counter, the espresso machine, the chalkboard menu. The barista — new one, moon tattoo, not Eli. The two women with their laptop. The man with his book.
Then, at the corner table, she drew herself.
Not a self-portrait. Just a shape — dark hair, hunched shoulders, hands around a cup. A presence. Evidence.

She drew Eli behind the counter. Round face, warm smile, the way he'd lean on his elbows when business was slow and ask her how the drawing was going. She drew him in careful lines, filling in details from memory — the small scar on his chin, the way his apron was always crooked.
When she finished, she looked at the napkin. Two figures — one at the counter, one at the corner table. A conversation across space. The ghost of a coffee shop that no longer existed.
She left the napkin on the table and walked out.
The bell didn't ring.

Outside, the cold hit her immediately. She pulled her jacket tighter and walked without direction — left, then right, then left again, the city unfolding around her in its grid of brick and glass. Her footsteps made no sound on the pavement. A woman with a stroller swerved to avoid her at the last second, startled, as if she'd appeared from nowhere.
She probably had.

The coffee shop sat behind her, warm and bright, its windows glowing amber against the gray afternoon. Inside, the new barista would clear her table. He'd find the napkin with the drawing on it. He'd look at it for a second — two figures, one behind the counter, one at the corner — and he wouldn't know what it meant. Maybe he'd toss it. Maybe he'd pin it to the wall behind the register where the staff sometimes posted oddities.
Either way, she wouldn't know. She was already gone.
Her phone buzzed.
She stopped walking. Pulled it from her pocket. Unknown number. Same thread.
**You're not the first. There's a place. Come to the Underlayer. Station 7, midnight.**

She read it three times. The Underlayer — she knew the word. Slang for the network of abandoned subway tunnels beneath the old east line. Frequented by squatters, artists, people who'd fallen through the city's cracks. She'd heard stories. Never been.
*You're not the first.*
Someone else had gone through this. Was going through this. Had survived long enough to send these messages.
She typed: *Who are you?*
Delivered. Not read.
She typed: *What's at Station 7?*
Delivered. Not read.
She stared at the screen. The cursor blinked, waiting. Midnight was hours away.
She put the phone back in her pocket and kept walking.

The park was empty except for a man sleeping on a bench and a dog owner throwing a ball into the failing light. Wren sat on a bench near the fountain — the fountain was off for the season, just a concrete basin with a few dead leaves floating in the standing water — and pulled out her sketchbook.

She drew the fountain. Then the bench. Then the dog, mid-leap, its ears pinned back, the ball just out of reach. The drawing was good — quick and sure, the lines alive with motion. She'd been drawing her whole life and the skill hadn't left her, even if everything else had.

She tore the page out and left it on the bench. Someone would find it. Someone would look at it and wonder who'd drawn a dog catching a ball in an empty park at dusk. Maybe they'd keep it. Maybe they'd throw it away.
The not-knowing was the point. She was leaving traces now. Little paper anchors scattered across the city. Proof that someone had been here, had seen this, had cared enough to draw it.
It wasn't enough. She knew that. A napkin in a coffee shop and a sketch on a park bench were whispered arguments against a world that was shouting her out of existence. But they were what she had.

The light faded. The dog owner left. The man on the bench slept on. Wren sat in the dark and listened to the city breathe — the distant rattle of the elevated train, the slam of a car door somewhere behind her, the low hum of a streetlight flickering on.

She could feel the current in the streetlight. That faint vibration, like a note played just below the threshold of hearing. Her new sense — or whatever it was — had been growing since the first flicker. The electrical world was becoming legible to her, character by character. She couldn't read it yet, but she could feel the shapes of the letters.
Her phone sat heavy in her pocket. Station 7. Midnight.
She didn't answer. Not yet.
She walked home in the dark. Slipped through the lobby behind a delivery driver. Took the stairs. Glitch-pushed her apartment door open and felt the familiar drain, like blood leaving her head.
Inside, she ate stolen bread and stolen cheese standing at the counter. The apartment was quiet. The hum of the refrigerator was the only sound.

The napkin was gone from the coffee shop table now. The new barista had cleared it. Eli was nowhere in the schedule. The slot in the world labeled *Eli* had been filled by someone else, and the slot labeled *Wren's regular barista who knew her name* had been erased entirely.
If no one remembered you had existed, did you?
She brushed the crumbs from her hands and went to bed. The sheets were cold. The pillow smelled like her shampoo, which was still there, which was something, which was a thread she could hold.
Midnight came and went.
She didn't go to Station 7.
Not yet.

*The coffee shop was still there. The corner table was still there. But the shape that had sat in it — the dark-haired woman with the pen and the Americano — had been overwritten. A ghost in her own routine, haunting a haunt that no longer recognized her.*
*Someone out there knew what she was. That was something.*
*It wasn't enough.*
